coredata.dc:title: Preparation of ZnO/NiO-loaded flexible cellulose nanofiber film electrodes and their application to dye-sensitized solar cells
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Gemeda, Getachew Feyissa
Etefa, Habtamu Fekadu
Hsieh, Chin-Chih
Kebede, Mekuriaw Assefa
Imae, Toyoko
Yen, Yee-Wen
coredata.dc:description: Since flexible solar cells are demanded portable purposes, materials are requested to be light and soft. Hence the present work focused on the preparation of metal oxide-based flexible dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) using sustainable, eco-friendly TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (TOCNF) films as substrates and their performances were compared. When nanorod (NR), nanowire (NW), and nanoparticle (NP) of ZnO or NiO as photovoltaic materials and carbon dot (Cdot) and N719 dye as photosensitizers were used, the coating of ZnONP (50 wt%) incorporated Cdot on ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF substrate revealed the better photovoltaic conversion efficiency (PCE) (1.34%) and the lower charge transfer resistance property. A NiONP/Cdot-coated NiONW/TOCNF electrode also achieved a good PCE, but the hydrophobic treatment of TOCNF improved the efficiency to be 1.45%. Although the flexible working electrodes for DSSCs were successfully manufactured from a biodegradable and sustainable TOCNF, more effort is still required at least to achieve the performance (4.43%) of conventional non-biodegradable polyethylene terephthalate substrate.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide
Nickel oxide
Nanorod
Nanowire
Nanoparticle
Flexible dye-sensitized solar cell
TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofiber
Carbon dot

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photovoltaic characteristics of flexible ZnO/Cdot-based TOCNF film DSSCs
Photovoltaic characteristics of flexible NiO/Cdot-based TOCNF film DSSCs
Comparison of photovoltaic characteristics of flexible film DSSCs

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) (a) FESEM and (b) TEM of ZnONR, (c) photograph and (d) FESEM of ZnONR/TOCNF film, (e) FESEM and (f) TEM of ZnONW, (g) TEM of ZnONW/Cdot, (h) FESEM of ZnONW/TOCNF film, (i) FESEM and (j) TEM of ZnONP, (k) TEM of ZnONP/Cdot, (l) FESEM of ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONW/TOCNF film. (B) SEM of (a) bare ZnONR/TOCNF film, (b) coated by 100 mg ZnONP, (c) coated by 50 mg ZnONP/Cdot, and (d) coated by 100 mg ZnONP/Cdot. Insets in Figure A(c), A(h) and (B(d) indicate the flexibility aspect of films.

(A) FT-IR of ZnONR, ZnONW, ZnONP, Cdots, and their composites with Cdot and TOCNF, and (B) XRD of TOCNF, PPy/TOCNF, ZnONR/TOCNF, and ZnONW/TOCNF. (C) TGA curves of ZnO/Cdot-based TOCNF, PPy/TOCNF, and TOCNF films.

(A) UV-visible absorption spectra of aqueous solutions (100 mg/l) and (B) Tauc plots of (1) ZnONW, (2) ZnONP, (3) ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%), (4) Cdot(1:1.5), and (5) ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdot(1:1.5, 11 wt%). (C) Photoluminescence emission and excitation spectra of (a) Cdot(1:1.5) and (b) ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdot(1:1.5, 11 wt%). (D) Nyquist and (E) Bode phase plots of EIS of (i) ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/TOCNF and (ii) ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdots(1:1.5, 11 wt%)/TOCNF electrodes.
(A) TEM images of NiONW at different Na2CO3 concentrations (2.00–4.19 M). (B) FESEM images of NiONW at 2.94 M Na2CO3.

(A) FTIR absorption spectra and (B) XRD of TOCNF, NiONW, NiONP-NiONW/TOCNF, and NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF. (C) The transient photocurrent responses of NiO/TOCNF film, ITO/TOCNF film, ITO/ODA-modified TOCNF film, ITO/PET film, and ITO/glass. (D) Nyquist plots of EIS of NiONW/TOCNF film (blue) and NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF film (black).

(a) J-V curves and (b) PCE plots of (A) ZnONP-ZnONR/TOCNF film electrode at different amounts of ZnONP, (B) ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONR/TOCNF film electrode at different ratios of ZnONP/Cdot, (C) ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONR/TOCNF film electrode at different ethylenediamine/citric acid mole ratios and (D) ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONW/TOCNF film electrode.

(A) J-V curves and (B) PCE plots (a) at different amounts of ZnONP, (b) at different amounts of Cdot(1:1), and (c) at different citric acid:ethylenediamine mole ratios of ZnO/Cdot-ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF film electrode.

(A) I-V curves and PCE plots of NiONW/TOCNF film DSSC and diameters and lengths of NiONW at different concentrations of Na2CO3. I-V curves, PCE plots, and film illustrations of (B) NiONP-NiONW/TOCNF film DSSC at different masses of NiONP and (C) NiONP(150 mg)/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF film DSSC at various masses of Cdot, (D) I-V curves and PCE plots of NiONP(150 mg)/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF film DSSC at various masses of NiONW. (E) I-V curves of (a) ITO/ODA-TOCNF, (b) NiONP-ITO/ODA-TOCNF and (c) NiONP/Cdot-ITO/ODA-TOCNF film electrodes.

(A) J-V curves, (B) PCE plots, and film illustrations of (a) NiONP-ITO/PET film DSSCs at different annealing temperatures, (b) NiO/Cdot-ITO/PET film DSSCs at different contents of Cdot(1:1), and (c) NiO/Cdot(12.5 wt%)-ITO/PET film DSSCs at various citric acid:ethylenediamine molar ratios.
Comparison of photovoltaic efficiencies of flexible DSSCs constructed of varied materials.*.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Sc = spin coating, Dp = deposition, Db = doctoral blade, EBE = electron bean evaporation, SP = screen-printing, Ed = electrophoresis deposition (room temperature), ISE on sq = in-situ electrodeposition on squeegee method, RT = room temperature, UVO = ultraviolet ozone, PEDOT = poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene), Mc = mechanical compression, Hs = heat sink.

head.ce:title: Preparation of ZnO/NiO-loaded flexible cellulose nanofiber film electrodes and their application to dye-sensitized solar cells

National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Graduate Institute of Applied Science and Technology, National Taiwan University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since flexible solar cells are demanded portable purposes, materials are requested to be light and soft. Hence the present work focused on the preparation of metal oxide-based flexible dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) using sustainable, eco-friendly TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (TOCNF) films as substrates and their performances were compared. When nanorod (NR), nanowire (NW), and nanoparticle (NP) of ZnO or NiO as photovoltaic materials and carbon dot (Cdot) and N719 dye as photosensitizers were used, the coating of ZnONP (50 wt%) incorporated Cdot on ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF substrate revealed the better photovoltaic conversion efficiency (PCE) (1.34%) and the lower charge transfer resistance property. A NiONP/Cdot-coated NiONW/TOCNF electrode also achieved a good PCE, but the hydrophobic treatment of TOCNF improved the efficiency to be 1.45%. Although the flexible working electrodes for DSSCs were successfully manufactured from a biodegradable and sustainable TOCNF, more effort is still required at least to achieve the performance (4.43%) of conventional non-biodegradable polyethylene terephthalate substrate.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar cell devices are the promising tool to solve climate change problems and biofuel energy crises (Gangwar, Tripathi & Singh, 2021). Widely, indium tin oxide (ITO) and fluorine tin oxide (FTO) electrodes were used for dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). They are nonelastic, hard, heavy, and costly glass substrates. The scarcity of indium element on earth and the rise of environmental pollution during their production process are some limitations of using such substrates in DSSCs (Rabaia et al., 2021). Thus, nowadays, some researchers are focusing on improving the DSSC technology towards flexible, lightweight, miniaturized, cost-efficient, and non-glass substrates, because non-rigid electrodes are suitable for handling and rolling-up (Li et al., 2019).

Currently, a synthesized flexible poly(ethylene terephthalate) (PET) is used as a substrate to prepare working electrodes after coated by different semiconductive materials and assembled with platinum foil as working and counter electrodes, respectively, to fabricate flexible DSSC (Zhang & Konno, 2018). However, since non-biodegradable PET substrates are non-ecofriendly, the utilization of natural polymers like cellulose is more advanced and encouraged because this biopolymer is most abundant on earth, inexpensive, widely available, sustainable, and environmentally friendly (Gupta et al., 2019). Here, the flexible electrode made from cellulose nano-fibril film can be lighter and more pleasant to carry and serve as a replacement with marketed flexible electrodes. The production of a new type of flexible transparent film, that is, a cellulose nanofiber film made from sustainable wood pulps has been challenged (Do, Grijalvo, Imae, Garcia-Celma & Rodríguez-Abreu, 2021; Krathumkhet, Imae & Nophawan, 2022; Rahmawati, Shih & Imae, 2020). However, the non-conductive cellulose film can be converted into conductive substrate through the incorporation of conductive materials for using as a flexible photovoltaic electrode.

Various nanostructures (nanoparticles (NPs), nanowires (NWs), and nanorods (NRs)) of semiconductors, including ZnO, TiO2, and NiO, and their composites were employed to enhance the conductivity and effective electron injection of flexible photoelectrodes in DSSCs (Singh & Shougaijam, 2022). ZnONP has been taken notice due to its semi-conductivity, superior electrochemical stability, large excitation binding energy (0.60 eV), small bandgap of 3.37 eV (Hamdy et al., 2021), suitability for doping, and non-toxicity. Meanwhile, ZnONW is also one of the most important nanomaterials in recent nanotechnology researches (Nadporozhskaya, Kovsh, Paolesse & Lvova, 2022). The electrochemical deposition is one of convenient techniques for the preparation of ZnONW, ZnONR, and NiO because the preparation condition is mild, and the process control is accurate. As carbon dot (Cdot) is one allotrope of carbon group with a zero dimension of a few nanometers and an intense photoluminescence character (Laghari, Memon, Yar Khuhawer & Jahangir, 2022), they can improve the conductivity of electrodes in DSSC. Thus, nanocomposites of Cdot with ZnO and NiO have offered a new direction on efficiency improvement on ITO DSSCs by exploring the effect of Cdot, which exerts a distinct advantage in charge separation and charge recombination (Efa & Imae, 2019; Etefa, Imae & Yanagida, 2020; Geleta & Imae, 2021).

The cellulose nanofiber films are applicable for embedding conductive polymers, metal oxides, and carbon materials. Thus, conductive films can be prepared and utilized as electrodes for electric (electronic) devices like flexible supercapacitors, fuel cells, solar cells, and so on. In this study, cellulose nanofiber films were incorporated metal oxides and Cdot, and the energy conversion, that is, the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of flexible nanocomposite DSSCs consisting of nanomaterials of p-ZnO and n-NiO were compared. Effects of Cdot were also investigated at various contents of addition and the different nitrogen ratios in Cdot, the latter of which was adjusted by mixing amounts of raw materials (ethylenediamine and citric acid). Cdot has been reported to behave as a photosensitizer as well as N719, accelerate the positive electron transfer, and restrain the charge recombination (Etefa et al., 2020). Thus, this research will present a step on the way for application of cellulose nanofiber films to flexile electrode devices.

Sodium bromide (NaBr), sodium borohydride (NaBH4), zinc nitrate hexahydrate (98%), ethylenediamine (99%), ethylene glycol (EG, 99+%), potassium iodide (KI), sodium carbonate (99.5%), pyrrole, 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-1-piperidinyloxy radical (TEMPO, 98%), iodine (I2) and Rhodamine B were bought from Acros Organics (USA). An aqueous sodium hypochlorite solution (74.4%), and never dried wood pulp (bleached eucalyptus kraft pulp, cellulose 95–98 wt%, hemicellulose 2–5 wt%, and lignin < 0.5 wt%) were purchased from Shimakyu Pure Chemicals and Canary Corporation (Taiwan), respectively. Ruthenium 535 bisTBA (N719), ammonium persulfate (APS, 98%), and acetonitrile (99.5%) were purchased from Scharlau (Spain), MACRON fine chemicals (Sweden), and J.T. baker (Taiwan), respectively. All the solutions were prepared using ultra-pure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩcm (Yamato Millipore WT100).

Characterizations were performed by utilizing a transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL, Japan, 120 kV), a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, JEOL JSM-6500F, Japan), a Fourier transform infrared absorption spectrophotometer (FTIR, NICOLET 6700, Thermo Scientific, USA), an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker, D2 Phaser, USA), an ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectrophotometer (JASCO V-670, Japan), and a HITACHI photoluminescence spectrometer using a solution cell of 10 mm thickness. Electrochemical measurements (current-voltage (J-V) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS)) were conducted using a Zahner CIMPS-XPot Electrochemical Workstation (Germany) together with the THALES software package (Spot-26,366).

ZnONR was prepared via the hydrothermal method (Alam &, Hossain; Dhandapani et al., 2021). Zinc nitrate hexahydrate and hexamethylenetetramine (0.1 M) in water (25 ml) were reacted in an autoclave at 95 °C for 5 h. The product was filtrated using a cellulose acetate membrane (0.2 μm pore size) and washed with water and ethanol. ZnONW was also synthesized with the hydrothermal method (Alshehri, Lewis, Pleydell-Pearce & Maffeis, 2018). Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (0.046 M) and sodium carbonate (3.66 M) in water were reacted in an autoclave at 140
∘
C
for 6 h. The white product (ZnONW) was purified filtering with water and dried. ZnONP was prepared via the polyol solvent method (Efa & Imae, 2018). Sodium hydroxide (1 M) in ethylene glycol (25 ml) was added dropwise into Zinc nitrate hexahydrate (1 M) in ethylene glycol (25 ml) and then stirred at 80
∘
C
for 1 h. The white product was purified by water and ethanol with centrifugation.

Cdots were synthesized with the hydrothermal method (Devadas & Imae, 2017). The citric acid (1 g) and ethylenediamine (150, 300, 522, and 669 µl) were heated with water (60 ml) in an autoclave at 230 °C for 5 h. Then Cdots with a 1:0.5, 1:1, 1:1.5, and 1:2 molar ratio of citric acid to ethylenediamine were produced.

TEMPO-oxidized cellulose nanofiber (TOCNF) was prepared based on the TEMPO-mediated oxidation procedure (Shah & Imae, 2016; Krathumkhet, Ujihara & Imae, 2021). The brief procedure is the oxidation reaction of never dried pulp (50 g), sodium bromide (5 g), and TEMPO (800 mg, 533.3 mg/l) in water (1.5 l) with an aqueous sodium hypochlorite (NaClO) solution (130 ml) at alkaline (pH 10) condition, the reduction with NaBH4 for 4 h, and the ultra-sonication. The oxidation reaction was finalized when the solution pH does not decrease with time, and the ultrasonication was continued until the transparent dispersion of TOCNF was obtained. The final product (TOCNF) was stored in a refrigerator (4 °C) to use in the next process.

Composites of TOCNF with ZnONR or ZnONW (ZnONR/TOCNF or ZnONW/TOCNF) were prepared by in situ growing ZnONR or ZnONW on cellulose nanofiber gel. An aqueous TOCNF dispersion (0.4 wt%, 10 ml) was added ZnONR or ZnONW precursor, performed the autoclave treatment described above, and purified. Finally, ZnO/TOCNF film was sandwiched by the acrylic sheets and dried in an oven at 45 °C. On the reparation of layered composite films, the dispersion of ZnO (100 mg) with Cdot (60 mg) in water was filtrated on the wet TOCNF-ZnO film beforehand filtrated on a cellulose acetate membrane (0.2 μm pore size), washed with water and ethanol, and dried in an oven at 45 °C. The products are ZnO/Cdot-ZnO/TOCNF films consisting of different ZnO (ZnONR, ZnONW, and ZnONP). A ZnO-ZnO/TOCNF film without Cdot was also prepared through the similar procedure. The transparent film of TOCNF changed to cloudy or white after mixed with ZnO and to pale brown after further addition of Cdot.

NiONW was synthesized using the hydrothermal method (Nguyen et al., 2018; Zhang, Yan & Gu, 2021). Nickel chloride (NiCl2, 0.387 g) and different concentrations of sodium carbonate (NaCO3, 3.67, 3.42, 3.15, 2.95, 2.66 and 2.00 M) were sonicated in water (40 ml) and stirred. The green solutions were heated in an autoclave at 140 °C for 6 h. The product was filtered using a 0.2 μm cellulose acetate membrane and washed with water. NiONP and its composite with Cdot (NiONP/Cdot) were the same samples that have been previously prepared (Etefa et al., 2020). Wet NiONW/TOCNF film was prepared by following the hydrothermal synthesis method for dispersion added TOCNF (15 ml, 0.4 wt%) to the precursor solution of NiCl2 and NaCO3. The dispersions of NiONP, NiO/Cdot, and NiONP/Cdot/NiONW were deposited separately on the NiONW/TOCNF film, washed with water, and dried. The product film was pale grown-gray due to the contribution of NiO and Cdot.

Indium tin oxide nanoparticle (ITO) was prepared using the co-precipitation method (Jiang, Liu, Zhai & Liu, 2021; Su et al., 2022). For indium(III) chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3•4H2O, 2.6 g) and tin(IV) chloride pentahydrate (SnCl4•5H2O, 0.26 g) dissolved in water (10 ml) at 50 °C, an aqueous ammonia (5%, 1.4 ml) solution was added and stirred. After ethylene diamine (0.76 ml) was added, the precipitate was washed with water and ethyl alcohol, dried at 120 °C for 1 h, and calcined at 600 °C for 1 h. A dispersion of NiONP (100 mg) or NiONP/Cdot (100 mg) in water (0.4 ml) was stirred for 4 h, spin-coated using a spin coater (K-359SD-1 SPINNER, KYOWA RIKEN, Japan, 2000 rpm) on 1 × 1 cm2 area of ITO/PET and dried. The electrodes were annealed in an oven at 150 °C for 25 min. Film thickness was determined using a micrometer.

On the preparation of a counter electrode film consisting of polypyrrole (PPy) and TOCNF, PPy was in situ polymerized on TOCNF film (Wan, Jiao & Li, 2017; Krathumkhet et al., 2021): Pyrrole monomer (120 μl) was dropped on the TOCNF film (thickness of 21 μm, an area of 2 × 2 cm2), and a solution of ammonium persulfate (204 mg) in 1 M HCl (7.0 ml) was dropped on the pyrrole/TOCNF film in an ice bath (below
4
∘
C
). After the 3 h reaction, the PPy/TOCNF composite film was cleaned with water, dried, and deposited Pt by sputtering.

Rhodamine B (30 μl, 5 mM in methanol) or N719 (40 μl, 5 mM in ethanol) was dropped to cover a 1 cm2 area of the TOCNF composite working electrode (2 × 2 cm2). The DSSC was assembled by injecting an electrolyte solution (a mixture of 0.3 M KI and 0.06 M I2 in acetonitrile) into a space which was made from 23 μm tape spacer between a ZnO- or NiO-based TOCNF working electrode film and a Pt-spattered PPy/TOCNF counter electrode film which were clipped together. ITO/PET electrode-based DSSC cells were also prepared similarly.

The current-voltage (J-V) curves were measured with a monochromatic blue light LED source of a 479 nm wavelength, and the total PCE was calculated following the equation of PCE = Jsc Voc FF/Jph, where Jsc is the short-circuit current density, Voc is the open-circuit voltage, FF is the fill factor, and Jph is the source of stimulated light at 100 W/m2. The numerical value of PCE was calculated as an average of five times measurements and a statistical error was described with the average value. EIS was recorded at a frequency range of 10 kHz – 10 Hz using an impedance measuring mode on a solar simulator in the same instrument.

Morphologies of ZnONR, ZnONW, ZnONP, and their composites with Cdot and TOCNF were characterized by SEM and TEM images (Fig. 1
A). ZnONR showed a rod-like shape with ∼500 nm in diameter and more than 2.5 μm in length. Wire-shaped ZnO exhibited thinner and longer than ZnONR in diameter (around 50 nm) and length (several micrometers), respectively. ZnONR/TOCNF and ZnONW/TOCNF films revealed the coating by ZnONR and ZnONW on TOCNF, respectively, and these films are flexible. The variation of flexibility between both films is comparable in insets of Fig. 1 A(c) and (h). In the case of ZnONW/TOCNF, the bending of more than 20 times does not damage its original state, although the ZnONR/TOCNF film was broken down after bending repeatedly 5 times, because long and thin ZnONW has a better network connectivity with TOCNF. Moreover, since the ZnONW/TOCNF film was more transparent than the ZnONR/TOCNF film, the ZnONW/TOCNF film is superior as a photovoltaic electrode to the ZnONR/TOCNF film. ZnONP was a sphere with 10–20 nm size and did not change the size after added Cdot, although Cdot is not distinguished in a TEM image due to its smaller size (less than 2.5 nm) (Devadas & Imae, 2018). A ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONW/TOCNF film showed additional spheres of ZnONP besides wire shape.

Fig. 1 B shows SEM images of ZnONP/Cdot coated-ZnONR/TOCNF films. On visual inspection, the film became the uniformly compact and flat surface, different from the bare ZnONR/TOCNF film, when ZnONP or ZnONP/Cdot was loaded. However, beyond 200 mg ZnONP/Cdot, visible cracks of coating films appeared (Supporting Information Figure S1A). Figure S1B shows photographs of TOCNF and TOCNF-PPy films. After loading PPy, the TOCNF film changed from transparent to black color.

IR spectra of ZnO, Cdot, and their composites are shown in Fig. 2
A. Most IR bands of ZnONP and ZnONR are derived from ethylene glycol and hexamethylenetetramine, respectively, which were used in their synthesis processes, but a band around 450 cm−1 is due to the Zn-O stretching vibration mode. This band locates at 500 cm−1 in a spectrum of ZnONW. IR bands of Cdot were observed at 3404 cm−1 (OH stretching mode of carboxylic acid group), 3264 cm −1 (NH2 stretching mode of amine group), 2957 cm−1 (CH stretching mode of aliphatic group), 1658 cm−1 (C=O stretching mode of carboxylic acid group), 1556 cm−1 (NH bending mode of amine group/CC stretching of graphitic group), and 1375 cm−1 (CC stretching mode of graphitic group) (Devadas & Imae, 2018; Efa & Imae, 2018; Geleta & Imae, 2021). Then FTIR spectra of ZnONP/Cdot and ZnONW/Cdot indicated the coexistence of both IR bands of Cdot and ZnO. The composite formation was similarly proved even on IR absorption spectra of ZnONR/TOCNF and ZnONW/TOCNF.

XRD patterns of TOCNF and its composites with ZnONW, ZnONR, and PPy are shown in Fig. 2 B. TOCNF has three obvious Bragg peaks at 2θ = 14.9, 15.6 and 23.6°, which can be assigned to crystallographic planes of cellulose crystal structure (Ren et al., 2022). PPy/TOCNF film displayed similar Bragg peaks of TOCNF, but a peak of PPy at 2θ = 24.0° of (104) plane (Li et al., 2022) was overlapped with a peak of TOCNF. A ZnONR/TOCNF film showed weak Bragg peaks of TOCNF below 2θ = 30° and simultaneously Bragg peaks of ZnO with wurtzite structure (JCPDS No 36–1451) at 2θ = 30 – 80°. A ZnONW/TOCNF film showed extremely strong peaks corresponding to wurtzite ZnO structure from 2θ = 30 – 80°, so that peaks of cellulose component were too weak to detect.

In TGA results (Fig. 2 C), different from TOCNF and PPy/TOCNF which revealed the complete decomposition at 1000 °C through three steps, ZnO-including TOCNF films displayed the slight weight loss (up to 5 wt%) till 200 °C and the second loss till 300–500 °C and kept constant weight till 1000 °C. The first step is the loss of adsorbed solvents and small molecules, and the second loss is of organic molecules including TOCNF. The residues are ZnO, and their amount depends on composites. The weight ratio between TOCNF and ZnONR was 1:1.6 for ZnONR/TOCNF and ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONR/TOCNF but increased more for ZnONW/Cdot-ZnONR/TOCNF (1:2.0), for ZnONP-ZnONR/TOCNF (1:2.7), and furthermore for ZnONP/Cdot-ZnONW/TOCNF. Finally, ZnONW/TOCNF had the highest weight ratio (1:7.2). These variations of ZnO contents should influence on the photovoltaic characters of ZnO.

Fig. 3
A shows UV–visible absorption spectra of ZnONW, ZnONP, ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%), Cdot(1:1.5), and ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdot(1:1.5, 11 wt%). These particles had absorption bands at 376, 346, 357, 340, and 343 nm, respectively. The absorption edge values obtained from a Tauc plot
(Fig. 3 B) were 2.80, 3.10, 3.05, 3.26, and 3.08 eV, respectively. ZnONW had the smallest band gap superior to ZnONP and Cdot, and composites displayed the interlevel band gap between components. Thus, the low band gap of ZnONW, as well as its larger content, make ZnONW expected a higher photovoltaic efficiency.

Fig. 3 C compares photoluminescence spectra of Cdot and ZnONW/ZnONP/Cdot. Cdot had two excitation bands at 245 and 343 nm and their corresponding emission band was at 439 nm, but the excitation (243, ∼340, and 374 nm) and emission (442 nm) bands of ZnONW/ZnONP/Cdot were weaker than those of Cdot. The decrease of fluorescence of Cdot indicates the energy transfer from Cdot to ZnO (Geleta & Imae, 2021).

To get the information regarding the effect of addition of Cdot, electron transfer behaviors of ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/TOCNF and ZnONW/ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdot(1:1.5, 11 wt%)/TOCNF electrodes were examined using EIS. Figs. 3 D and 3 E show Nyquist and Bode phase plots of both electrodes. The charge transfer resistance, Rct, the ohmic series resistance, Rs, and the electron lifetime were obtained based on a Bandle's equivalent circuit and listed in Table S1. Both resistances decreased 6.3 and 83.2 Ω, respectively, and the electron lifetime increased 17.4 ms after the addition of Cdot. These results indicate that the addition of Cdot accelerates the charge transfer in ZnO semiconductors, being consistent with decreases of band gap and fluorescence. These tendencies are preferable to enhance the photovoltaic performance in addition of Cdot (Efa & Imae, 2018).

NiONW was synthesized at different Na2CO3 concentrations (2.00 – 4.19 M) using the hydrothermal method (Su, Kim, Kim & Wang, 2012). As seen in TEM images, the wire-like shape of NiONW with diameters of 30–100 nm and lengths of 1–5 μm was observed (Fig. 4
(A)). FESEM image of NiONW (2.94 M Na2CO3) also exhibited the wire shape of NiO (Fig. 4 (B)).

In Fig. 5
A, IR absorption bands of NiONW at 3392,1624 and 447 cm−1 are attributed to OH stretching, OH bending, and Ni-O stretching vibration modes. OH bands originate from OH group on the ZnO surface or coexisting water. IR bands of both NiONW and TOCNF appeared in absorption spectra of NiONP-NiONW/TOCNF and NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF films, although a band at 1642 cm−1 attributable to OH bending mode in NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF overlapped with a 1651 cm−1 (C=O stretching) band of Cdot. Incidentally, ITO has an IR band below 750 cm−1 (Figure S2A).

To confirm the production of composites, XRD patterns were measured as shown in Fig. 5 B. Common diffraction peaks of NiONW, NiONP-NiONW/TOCNF and NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF were (111), (200), (220), (311), and (222), which are the same indexes as diffraction peaks of NiONP cubic structure (Devadas & Imae, 2018). The XRD pattern of TOCNF film showed a peak with an index of (002) but did not show remarkable peaks above 2θ = 30° (see Fig. 2 B).

Electrical characteristics of NiO-based films were evaluated using the transient photocurrent response for different electrodes. Figure S2B shows response profile versus time and the obtained current response is plotted in Fig. 5 C. The current response increased in the order of NiO/TOCNF film (0.3 mAcm−2) < ITO/TOCNF film (0.6 mAcm−2) < ITO/ODA-modified TOCNF film (0.75 mAcm−2) < ITO/PET film (1.05 mAcm−2) < ITO/glass (1.2 mAcm−2). Thus, a PET film was superior to TOCNF film for a current flow. Nyquist plots of EIS are plotted in Fig. 5 D. Charge transfer resistance of NiONW/TOCNF and NiONP/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF were 43.51 and 32.03 Ω, respectively, and these values were higher than that (21.45 Ω) of NiO/glass.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic characteristics of flexible ZnO/Cdot-based TOCNF film DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Flexible DSSC cells were prepared from an anode electrode of Rhodamine B-adsorbed ZnO/Cdot-ZnO/TOCNF film, a cathode electrode of PPy/TOCNF film, and an electrolyte solution of KI and I2 in acetonitrile. Then J-V curves were measured and PCE values were calculated (Fig. 6 ). When ZnONP was loaded on ZnONR/TOCNF, the PCE increased with increasing ZnONP amount but it was only 0.051% at 100 mg ZnONP content. However, when ZnONP/Cdot was loaded on ZnONR/TOCNF film and the CA:EDA ratio in Cdot was varied, the PCE changed. The PCE for ZnONP/Cdot(1:1.5, 37.5 wt%) was 0.18%. If ZnONW was used instead of ZnONP, the PCE increased to be 0.25%. Moreover, when ZnONP/TOCNF film was replaced with ZnONW/TOCNF film and ZnONP/Cdot(1:1.5, 37.5 wt%) was coated, the PCE was the highest (0.40%). Numerical values are listed in Table S2.

It was confirmed that ZnONW/TOCNF film was better than ZnONR/TOCNF film and the coating with ZnONW/Cdot was better than with ZnONP/Cdot. However, these PCE values are too small compared with those of ZnONP/Cdot on ITO substrate which have been reported previously (Efa & Imae, 2019). One of reasons is the photosensitizer, because rhodamine B is a less effective sensitizer than N719 (Efa & Imae, 2019). There is a report that the calcination of ZnO ITO cathod is preferable on the absorption of photosensitizer but such procedure was not effective on TOCNF-based flexible electrodes, since the electrochemical performance decreased after calcination because the calcination affects the shape change or the enlarging of ZnO to make the charge transport more difficult and decrease the conductivity (data are not shown).

The morphology of ZnO influences on photovoltaic performance. ZnONPs with small size (20 nm) provide a suitable surface area for dye adsorption. Thus, the PCE of flexible DSSCs applied ZnONPs as photoanodes increases. The length of ZnONRs is shorter than ZnONWs for constructing the electron channel but larger than ZnONPs with larger surface area. ZnONWs with suitable length can provide a direct pathway for rapid transport of electrons on the conducting surface of the photoanode, because ZnONWs have a low band gap, large amount loading on TOCNF, good transparency, and better network connection between the wire. Thus, it can be confirmed that the electrode constructed by coating ZnONPs on ZnONW-embedded TOCNF produces the better PCE (0.138%). The similar layer arrangement of electrode was adopted even for the NiO-based DSSC.

Thus, photovolataic measurements were performed using an N719 photosensitizer and working anode electrodes of ZnONP-coated flexible ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF composite films, keeping the same cathode electrode and an electrolyte solution. Fig. 7 and Table S3 show J-V curves, parameters from J-V curves, and film illustrations. When different amounts (0–75 wt%) of ZnONP were deposited on a ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF film electrode, DSSC provided the highest 0.138% PCE at 50 wt% ZnONP. This result indicates that the inclusion of ZnONP raises 2 times in maximum of PCE (0.073%) of the ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF film electrode without deposited ZnONP.

For ZnONP(50 wt%)-ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF film, when different amounts of Cdots(1:1) was mixed to ZnONP(50 wt%), the current density increased up to 11 wt% content of Cdot(1:1) and decreased at higher content of Cdot(1:1). Consequently, the PCE was highest (1.15%) at 11 wt% Cdots(1:1). The photovoltaic properties and parameters from J-V curves of ZnONP(50 wt%)/Cdots(11 wt%)-ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF flexible film electrode were examined as a function of citric acid:ethylenediamine (mole ratio) in Cdot. J-V curves of flexible DSSCs were meaningfully influenced on mole ratio of citric acid:ethylenediamine and parameters were commonly highest at 1:1.5. Thus the PCE was maximized at 1.34 ± 0.01%. Due to the adsorption on ZnO materials, Cdot can be simply incorporated into ZnO-based composites and increased the surface area and the light absorption of composites (Efa & Imae, 2019). The interaction between Cdot and ZnO may contribute to the enhancement of charge separation, the reduction of charge recombination, and the increase of charge transport on ZnO materials which are important for the photocurrent generation (Mahala, Sharma & Basu, 2020) Figure S3 and Table S4 illustrate the PCE of ZnONP (50 wt%) on ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF substrate. The PCE before and after bending were 1.33 ± 0.02% and 1.31 ± 0.03%, respectively. There are scarce differences in efficiency, demonstrating that the bendability does not jeopardize the performance of device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photovoltaic characteristics of flexible NiO/Cdot-based TOCNF film DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: It has been reported that NiO/Cdot on ITO provides more effective photovoltaic performance (PCE: 9.85%) than ZnO/Cdot on ITO (Efa & Imae, 2019; Geleta & Imae, 2021). Thus, the comparison of ZnO and NiO also should be examined in the present flexible electrode work. In this case, although NiO is a p-type semiconductor different from n-type ZnO semiconductor, the cathode electrode was prepared with NiONP-NiO/TOCNF and NiONP/Cdot-NiO/TOCNF films, and the anode electrode was Pt-deposited PPy/TOCNF film.

NiONW/TOCNF film electrode DSSC prepared at 2.94 M content of NaCO3 in TOCNF displayed the highest PCE (0.48%) (see Fig. 8
A and Table S5). Then NiONW had the shortest diameter (30–50 nm) and longest length (3–5 μm). Meanwhile, when NiONP was deposited on NiONW/TOCNF film, PCE values decreased like the highest 0.20% for 150 mg mass amount of NiONP deposition (see in Fig. 8 B and Table S5). However, when the composite consisting of Cdot and NiONP (NiONP/Cdot) was deposited on NiONW/TOCNF film substrate, the PCE of DSSC increased to a maximum of 1.25% at citric acid:ethylenediamine of 1:1.5 mole ratio in Cdot (see in Fig. 8C and Table S5). Because the smoothness and uniformity of the film were observed after the addition of Cdot, the generated smooth photo-current surface may result in the effectiveness of the electrode. In addition, the adsorption of Cdot on NiONP can help to increase the charge separation and reduce the charge recombination, thereby leading the photo-current generation (Etefa et al., 2020). The effect of hydrophobicity of TOCNF was also examined. Fig. 8 E and Table S5 show J-V curves of ODA-treated TOCNF-based DSSC and analyzed parameters. PCE values of ITO/ODA-TOCNF, NiONP-ITO/ODA-TOCNF, and NiONP/Cdot-ITO/ODA-TOCNF film electrodes were 0.019, 0.24, and 1.45%, respectively. These values were higher than the corresponding DSSCs with unmodified TOCNF.

For comparison, the ITO-coated PET film was used instead of the conductive TOCNF film. J-V curves of NiONP-ITO/PET film working electrode DSSCs at different annealing temperatures were displayed in Fig. 9
(a), and parameters were calculated as listed in Table S6. DSSCs had the highest PCE at 150 °C, and the value (1.73 ± 0.15%) was higher than 1.25% of NiONP(150 mg)/Cdot-NiONW/TOCNF film electrode. However, the PCEs decreased at temperatures higher than 150 °C, because of the deformation of the polymer film. When NiONP/Cdot was deposited on the ITO/PET film substrate, a maximum PCE was 2.57% at 12.5 wt% Cdot(1:1) (Fig. 9 (b) and Table S6). Moreover, the varied mole ratios of citric acid:ethylenediamine impacted J-V curves and PCE values, as shown in Fig. 9 (c) and Table S6. J-V curves on the ITO/PET film substrate demonstrated the preferable result (maximum 4.43% PCE) at a 1:1.5 molar ratio of citric acid:ethylenediamine, suggesting that the amine (NH2) component in Cdot influences on the DSSC performance (Etefa et al., 2020), because amine in Cdot is efficient to change the electrical and optical properties of metal oxide through the shifting of energy gap (Efa & Imae, 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of photovoltaic characteristics of flexible film DSSCs
ce:section.ce:para: Table 1 compares PCE performance of flexible DSSCs using various electrode materials as flexible working substrates, ITO-coated PET and polyethylene naphthalate (PEN) have been mainly used. In opposition to non-biodegradable PET and PEN, sustainable and environmentally beneficial TOCNFs were taken conductivity by adding conductive ZnO or NiO in the present work. When semiconductive ZnO or TiO2 was coated on conductive PET and PEN substrates, PCEs of these flexible DSSCs reached 4.0–4.4% (Xue et al., 2014; Baiju, Murali, Rao, Jayanarayanan & Kumaresan, 2020). These values were higher than PCEs of conductive TOCNF-based DSSCs. The highest PCE values of ZnONP/Cdot- and NiONP/Cdot-coated conductive TOCNF film electrodes were 1.34 ± 0.01 and 1.30 ± 0.01%, respectively, indicating the similarity of PCEs for both electrodes. However, in NiO-based DSSC, ITO/ODA-TOCNF film substrate was more effective (PCE = 1.45 ± 0.04%) than NiONW/TOCNF film substrate, because ODA-TOCNF is more hydrophobic than TOCNF. In comparison, the ITO/PET film substrate generated a higher PCE value (4.43 ± 0.13%) than the TOCNF-based film substrate. This value is comparable or slightly higher than that (4.11–4.42%) reported previously (Xue et al., 2014; Baiju et al., 2020). Thus, as shown in Table 1, the TOCNF-based flexible substrate used in flexible solar cells is less effective than the ITO/PET film substrate, and further improvement is expected.

The demand of getting electric energy is considerably increasing because of the increasing amount of the global population as well as the accretive plenty of human life on technologies that involve the help of electric power in our day-to-day life. The challenge in the present investigation is the manufacturing of lightweight, flexible, folding, and integrated electronic generation nano-systems, where the efficient conversion from solar light energy into electric energy was adopted with the help of photovoltaic devices because the solar energy of sunlight is a pollution-free and highly renewable resource.

The ZnONP/Cdot-coated ZnONW/Cdot/TOCNF film DSSC exhibited the similar PCE value to the NiONP/Cdot-coated NiONW/TOCNF film DSSC. However, although the ODA-treated hydrophobic TOCNF offered higher PCE than the hydrophilic pristine TOCNF, these TOCNF-based electrodes still displayed weaker PCE values than the NiONP/Cdot-coated ITO/PET film electrode and must be regulated the thickness, smoothness and homogeneity of films for improving the photocurrent density and electrical conductivity of substrates to revise the performance of DSSC. Nevertheless, the potential of the TOCNF-based flexible electrode is low cost, environmentally friendly, and simple production, making it applicable to a wide range of applications on the electrochemistry.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was partly supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan (MOST 108–2221-E-011–105-). GFG, HFE and MAK give thanks to the National Taiwan University of Science and Technology for kind financial support by a student scholarship and postdoctoral fellowship.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.carpta.2022.100213.
